name: Warp (Rust Web Framework)

agent_readiness.notes: Filter-based composable web framework on top of hyper. use warp::Filter. let hello = warp::path!('hello' / String).map(|name| format!('Hello, {}!', name)). warp::serve(hello).run(([127,0,0,1], 3030)).await. Filters compose: let api = warp::path('api').and(warp::post()).and(warp::body::json()).and_then(handler). JSON: warp::body::json::<T>(). warp::reply::json(&obj). Query params: warp::query::<Params>(). CORS: warp::cors().allow_any_origin(). WebSocket: warp::ws(). Static files: warp::fs::dir(). Rejection handling: .recover(handle_rejection). Type-level routing: each filter is a type — complex types from deep composition can be painful. Slower compile times than Axum.

gotchas: Filter composition creates deeply nested types — error messages are very long
Slow compilation for complex filter chains
Less active development than Axum — Axum is now preferred in tokio ecosystem
best_when: Simple REST APIs with few routes
When warp's composable filter pattern matches the mental model
avoid_when: New projects — prefer Axum (same ecosystem, better ergonomics)
Complex routing with many middlewares (type errors become unmanageable)
summary: Warp is a filter-based Rust web framework on hyper. warp::path!('hello' / String).map(|name| ...). Filters compose with .and(). warp::serve(routes).run(addr).await. JSON, WebSocket, static files, CORS. MIT by seanmonstar. Less active than Axum. Complex type errors from deep filter composition. Slow compile times. Best for existing warp codebases; new projects should prefer Axum.
